dialogue.utterance: Unit 3, responding for a report of breathing problems at --Address Redacted--.
Hi, I'm Jermaine, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I can't catch my breath. It's been this way since I woke up.
Understood. This is classified as a breathing problem. Have you had chest pain or any other symptoms along with the difficulty breathing?
Can you tell me your name and where you are right now?
I'm Gertrude and I'm at my apartment.
Can you tell me the current date and time?
Today is the 14th, and it's early morning. I had this since about 6 this morning.
Can you nod your head if you're breathing on your own?
Nods.
Your breathing is very fast and shallow. I'm going to give you high-flow oxygen right now to help.
Airway is patent and she's breathing independently.
We've placed you on a non-rebreather with 15 liters per minute.
I was already on it. The firefighters placed it earlier.
Pulse is 135 and regular.
Breathing is at 30 per minute, shallow, and there's no obvious airway obstruction.
Blood pressure is 212 over 137-quite elevated.
Oxygen saturation is 88% on 15L non-rebreather.
EKG shows sinus tachycardia at this time.
Glucose reading is not completed-patient was not accessible for testing.
Gertrude, since 6 this morning, has anything else noticeable come along with the shortness of breath?
No other symptoms besides the difficulty breathing.
What medications are you currently taking?
Lasix, Lipitor, Synthroid, Cymbalta, Neurontin, aspirin, and Remeron.
And can you provide your age and any significant medical conditions?
I'm 70 years old. I have COPD, congestive heart failure, emphysema, high blood pressure, and peripheral vascular disease.
I'm placing you on a 4-lead ECG and continuous monitoring.
We're initiating CPAP at 5 cm H2O to help with your breathing.
Yes, let's go ahead.
I'm going to attempt an IV in your left arm for potential medication access if needed.
Alright, do it.
That IV attempt was unsuccessful and will remain a pending task en route.
Gertrude experienced restlessness and stated, "I'mma pass out," but has improved with CPAP.
We're proceeding under protocol 3-11 for Medical-Respiratory Distress.
A 12-lead EKG was completed and shows some ST elevation in V1, V2, V3-too minimal for a STEMI alert.
Breathing has become slower at 28 per minute with increased depth and more controlled effort.
Pulse is now 126 and remains regular.
Blood pressure is 193 over 74, still high.
Oxygen saturation has improved to 96%.
On a scale from 1 to 10, how would you rate any pain or discomfort you're feeling?
I feel no pain, just a bit short of breath.
Chippenham Hospital is better suited for your care-we'll head there with lights and sirens.
That works.
Gertrude is a left foot amputee and partial right foot amputee. We'll use a limb-lift technique.
You remain alert and oriented the whole time.Hang in there.
Your skin is cool, pale, and dry-nothing changed with symptoms.
Gertrude is now secured on the stair chair and ready for safe transport.
She is transferred using three straps; bed rails have been raised for extra safety.
Gertrude is now secured and moving safely down the stairs with limb-lift assistance.
Gertrude is placed on the ambulance cot and secured with five straps.
Bed rails have been raised to ensure extra security on the cot.
Non-rebreather is now switched to CPAP at 5 cm/H2O for continued support.
The patient has no pain at this point.
Earlier, she mentioned feeling lightheaded but has since calmed with CPAP.
Gertrude, I know you're not feeling 100%, but you're getting the best care possible.
A brief patient report has been called into Chippenham using my personal cell phone. Ready to provide more verbal details.
Gertrude is a 70-year-old female with known medical history of COPD, CHF, emphysema, and PVD.
Initial vitals: BP 212/137, pulse 135, respirations 30, oxygen saturation 88%. Post CPAP, BP 193/74, pulse 126, respirations 28, oxygen 96%.
12-lead EKG shows some ST elevation in V1, V2, V3 but doesn't meet STEMI alert criteria.
IV access was unsuccessful. No other symptoms reported besides respiratory distress.
She's been on CPAP for most of transport and is calm with reassured breathing.
STEMI alert was not activated, and the patient reported no chest or cardiac complaints.
There were no complications during transport using the limb-lift and cot straps.
A full report has been sent to Chippenham ED using my personal phone. A sheet pull is required.
The patient has no drug allergies and has all current medications on file.
She is now ready for hospital staff to receive her and continue current care.
No distress was observed during transport from our team. Willing to answer questions.
I'll stay back and assist on handoff so there's a smooth transition.
